title: Cat Stiff Gait When Waking Up: Causes, Urgency, and Care Guide
meta_desc: Learn why your cat might have a stiff gait after sleeping, the urgency of the situation, and how to help your feline friend feel better.
content_html:
TL;DR: A stiff gait in cats after resting is often a sign of osteoarthritis and usually isn't an emergency unless they suddenly cannot walk or are in extreme distress.

Why does my cat walk stiffly when they first wake up?

  • Stiffness often indicates that your cat's joints are feeling tight or painful after periods of inactivity.
  • This is common in senior cats and is frequently a sign of feline osteoarthritis or degenerative joint disease.
  • The stiffness often improves after a few minutes of walking as the synovial fluid in the joints begins to circulate better.

Is my cat's stiff gait a medical emergency?

  • Low urgency: If the stiffness resolves with movement, it is generally a sign of chronic discomfort that should be addressed during a scheduled veterinary appointment.
  • Immediate emergency: Seek care immediately if your cat suddenly cannot walk at all, drags their hind legs, or seems in extreme distress.

How can recording a video of my cat help the veterinarian with triage?

  • Overcomes masking: Cats often hide their pain or act differently at the vet clinic due to stress or adrenaline.
  • Captures the onset: Recording your cat the moment they stand up from a nap allows your vet to see the exact nature of the lameness in a natural home environment.
  • Aids diagnosis: This visual evidence helps the medical team distinguish between muscular stiffness, neurological issues, or joint-specific pain.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Lameness in cats, manifesting as a stiff gait particularly upon waking, can stem from various causes, including fibrotic myopathy of the semitendinosus muscle or restrictions due to annular ligaments, adhesions, or severe fibrosis. Pain-related lameness can be weight-bearing or nonweight-bearing. Diagnosis is crucial as treatment varies significantly depending on the underlying cause. For example, mechanical lameness, will not respond to analgesics, whereas lameness caused by pain often responds to systemic or local analgesics and anti-inflammatory drugs. Doppler ultrasonography helps evaluate blood flow. Management includes analgesics, nursing care, and treatment of any underlying disease. Neurologic deficits may improve within weeks, but complete recovery may take months, and permanent deficits are possible.

Chapter: Neurology, Orthopedics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1096)

faqs.question: Is my cat in pain if they are walking stiffly?
faqs.answer: Yes, stiffness is typically a physical manifestation of discomfort. Cats are masters at hiding pain, so a stiff gait is a subtle sign that they are managing chronic soreness.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human pain relievers?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your cat human medications like Tylenol or Aspirin. These are highly toxic to cats and can be fatal. Only use medications prescribed by your veterinarian.

faqs.question: At what age do cats usually start getting stiff?
faqs.answer: While it varies, many cats begin showing signs of joint stiffness or arthritis around age 7 to 10. It becomes much more prevalent in senior cats over the age of 12.
